text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Margaret Okonkwo
DOB: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Division: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
Routine oncology follow-up for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Review of restaging CT imaging obtained prior to today's visit. Ongoing management of cabozantinib-related toxicities. Disease status assessment at 6 months on second-line therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with Stage IV clear cell RCC (cT3a N1 M1, ISUP grade 3 with 20% sarcomatoid differentiation), initially diagnosed approximately 15 months ago. Her primary disease presented as a large right renal mass (11.4 cm) with retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary metastases. She underwent a right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection roughly 13-14 months ago — final pathology confirmed pT3a pN1 M1 clear cell RCC with 5/8 positive nodes. Molecular profiling (FoundationOne CDx) showed the expected VHL and PBRM1 alterations, as well as BAP1 loss (consistent with sarcomatoid features), SETD2 frameshift, TP53 R248W, and CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion. PD-L1 CPS was 18 on surgical specimen.

She completed 4 induction cycles of ipi 1 mg/kg + nivo 3 mg/kg (Checkmate 214 regimen) followed by nivolumab 480 mg maintenance every 4 weeks. Her best response on IO was a mixed/partial response — meaningful shrinkage in the dominant right lower lobe nodule and left upper lobe lesion, though the smaller nodules were essentially stable throughout. She also experienced Grade 2 immune-related colitis (managed with oral prednisone taper, one cycle of ipi held) and Grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism. She was started on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, which she continues. Unfortunately, at approximately 9 months after IO initiation, restaging CT demonstrated clear progression — all four pulmonary nodules were growing, two new small right middle lobe nodules appeared, and two new small right hepatic lobe enhancing lesions were identified. Nivolumab was discontinued.

She was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib at 60 mg daily approximately 6 months ago. Within the first 3-4 weeks she developed Grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (HFS) and Grade 2 diarrhea, necessitating a dose reduction to 40 mg daily. Amlodipine was uptitrated to 10 mg for Grade 2 TKI-related hypertension at around 6 weeks. She has been maintained at cabo 40 mg since. Her restaging CT at 3 months on cabo showed a modest decrease in most target lesions, which was felt to represent stable to minor response — not quite hitting the threshold for a partial response, but directionally favorable and no new sites of disease. Today she presents for her 6-month cabozantinib follow-up visit with repeat CT results in hand.

In terms of current symptoms, she reports persistent but manageable fatigue — she would characterize it as moderate, perhaps 4-5/10 on a severity scale, most pronounced in the afternoons. She has been going for short walks in her neighborhood and has been engaged with integrative oncology for exercise guidance. She notes her feet remain somewhat tender bilaterally, particularly on the heels and balls of the feet, but she is not blistering. She is using the urea 40% cream twice daily and wears cushioned, wide-toe-box shoes consistently. She continues to have 1-2 loose stools per day above her prior baseline, mostly in the morning, which she manages with loperamide PRN (roughly 3-4 times per week). Her appetite is fair — she has lost approximately 4 kg since starting cabozantinib, which was noted at the prior visit, though she reports her weight has been fairly stable over the last 6-8 weeks. She denies nausea, vomiting, hemoptysis, chest pain, or dyspnea at rest. No new bone pain or neurological symptoms. She notes mild cold sensitivity ongoing, attributed to her levothyroxine-managed hypothyroidism — she feels her energy around thyroid function is improved compared to when the hypothyroidism was first identified.

ECOG performance status today: 1. She came to clinic alone and walked in without difficulty.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue as above. Weight stable x 6-8 weeks. No fevers, chills, or night sweats.
Pulmonary: No hemoptysis, no new dyspnea, no cough. Mild exertional breathlessness with brisk walking (unchanged from prior).
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: 1-2 loose stools/day above baseline, managed with loperamide PRN. No blood in stool, no significant cramping. Appetite fair.
Genitourinary: Urinary frequency unchanged (single right kidney status post-nephrectomy; adequate urine output reported). No flank pain.
Musculoskeletal: Bilateral foot tenderness (plantar), Grade 1, no significant hand involvement. Mild arthralgias in knees and hips, stable, partially responsive to naproxen PRN (used 2-3x/week).
Skin: Bilateral foot skin changes as above, Grade 1 — no blistering, no significant erythema at this time. No new rashes.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal weakness, no sensory changes.
Endocrine: Cold intolerance mild, energy improved. No symptoms of hyperthyroidism.
Psychosocial: She reports moderate adjustment difficulties but found the social work referral helpful. She is in contact with her daughter frequently. No active depression screen concerns today per PHQ-2 (score 1).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: BP 136/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 97% on room air. Weight 67.2 kg (prior visit 67.0 kg — essentially stable).

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversational.
HEENT: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear. Mild dryness of oral mucosa noted — asked to increase hydration.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Right flank surgical scar well-healed. No hepatomegaly palpated, though liver dullness appreciated on percussion. No splenomegaly.
Extremities: Bilateral plantar surfaces with mild hyperkeratosis and faint erythema, more prominent at heel pads and metatarsal heads — consistent with Grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. No active blistering. Palms show residual faint erythema, not tender to palpation.
Skin: No new rashes, purpura, or lesions of concern.
Neurological: Grossly intact. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained within 48 hours of visit)

CBC: WBC 6.2 K/uL, Hgb 10.6 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable from prior 10.8 g/dL), platelets 214 K/uL.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.9 mEq/L, Cl 102 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L. BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (stable, eGFR ~43 mL/min/1.73m2 — stable from prior). ALT 36 U/L, AST 31 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, alk phos 92 U/L (all within normal limits). Total protein 6.9 g/dL, albumin 3.7 g/dL (reassuringly near normal).
TSH: 2.6 mIU/L — within normal range, stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily.
Lipase: 44 U/L (normal).
Urinalysis: trace proteinuria, no hematuria — stable, monitored given single kidney and TKI.
INR/PT: not checked today.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained prior to today's visit, approximately 6 months after cabo initiation, 15 months from original diagnosis):

Chest: The right lower lobe lesion is stable at roughly 1.8 cm — unchanged from the prior scan. Left upper lobe nodule stable at 0.9 cm. Right upper lobe nodule essentially stable at approximately 1.0 cm. Left lower lobe nodule stable at 0.8 cm. The right middle lobe small nodules are unchanged. No new pulmonary lesions identified. No pleural effusion.

Abdomen/pelvis: The two hepatic lesions in the right lobe measure 0.7 and 0.5 cm — essentially unchanged from prior imaging, possibly very minimally smaller in the larger one. No new hepatic lesions. Nephrectomy bed is clean without evidence of local recurrence. No retroperitoneal adenopathy. No adrenal lesions. No ascites.

Bone windows: No new lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions.

Overall impression: Continued stable disease — all known sites of metastatic involvement are essentially unchanged. No new lesions have appeared. This represents durable disease control on cabozantinib, now confirmed at two consecutive restaging intervals (3 and 6 months).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic clear cell RCC (ISUP Grade 3, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation; BAP1-mutant, VHL/PBRM1/SETD2 co-altered), now 15 months from diagnosis and approximately 6 months on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily.

Today's restaging CT confirms ongoing stable disease across all pulmonary and hepatic sites of metastasis. This is a favorable result — cabo has provided durable disease control in a patient with aggressive biology (sarcomatoid features, BAP1 loss, TP53 mutation), and she remains PS 1. The two consecutive stable disease assessments at 3 and 6 months give us confidence that the disease is not smoldering toward progression. Plan to continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Next restaging CT in approximately 3 months.

2. Hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia), Grade 1 — stable at this dose level.

Current management with urea 40% cream BID and cushioned footwear appears effective at maintaining Grade 1. No dose modification warranted at this time. Encouraged to continue current preventive measures. Patient asked about ammonium lactate lotion as an alternative/adjunct — reasonable to try if urea cream is irritating, though she tolerates it well. Will reinforce avoidance of heat, friction, and tight footwear. If breakthrough Grade 2 recurrence, will hold cabo for 5-7 days and resume at 40 mg with reassessment.

3. Cabozantinib-associated diarrhea, Grade 1 — controlled.

Continue loperamide PRN. Low-residue dietary modifications ongoing. She is functioning well. No dose modification needed.

4. Hypertension — controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily.

BP 136/82 today — at goal. Continue amlodipine 10 mg. No change needed. Will recheck BP at next visit.

5. Immune-related hypothyroidism (sequela of prior nivolumab) — stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg.

TSH 2.6 mIU/L — well within range on current dose. Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose adjustment needed. Recheck thyroid function in 3 months.

6. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.6 g/dL) — stable, chronic, multifactorial (nephrectomy, TKI effect, possible chronic disease contribution).

Trend is stable over recent months. No evidence of active bleeding. Iron studies will be checked at next labs to rule out iron deficiency component — if deficient, oral iron supplementation considered. No erythropoiesis-stimulating agent indicated at this time. Fatigue counseling reinforced; integrative oncology exercise referral active.

7. Nutritional status and weight.

Weight stable over the last 6-8 weeks (67.2 kg today vs. 67.0 kg prior visit). Albumin 3.7 g/dL — acceptable. Total weight loss since starting TKI approximately 4 kg. Patient counseled on high-protein, calorie-dense diet. Will refer to oncology dietitian if further weight loss occurs.

8. Psychosocial support.

Social work engagement ongoing, patient finds it helpful. Palliative care consultation re-discussed — patient remains hesitant but is more open to a future "symptom management" framing rather than "end of life" framing. Will attempt a warm handoff referral to palliative care at next visit, framed around fatigue and quality of life optimization.

9. Goals of care.

Ms. Okonkwo continues to express a strong desire for active treatment. She understands the palliative intent of therapy in the metastatic setting. She is informed that cabo is providing meaningful disease control and that we will continue monitoring for tolerance and efficacy. She wishes to maintain quality of life at home and continue her activities.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months (or sooner if clinically indicated). Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be ordered approximately 2 weeks prior. Labs prior to each visit. Patient instructed to call with new or worsening symptoms.

Time spent: approximately 40 minutes, majority in counseling and care coordination.

[Signed: Attending, Genitourinary Medical Oncology]
